title: Designing a drip irrigation system from a water butt
subtitle: Planning a gravity-fed drip or soaker-hose system for vegetable beds
excerpt: A drip or soaker-hose system connected to a water butt delivers water directly to plant roots, reduces surface evaporation by 30 to 50 percent compared to overhead watering, and runs without any electricity. Gravity provides the pressure if the butt is raised 50 to 80 cm above the ground. A simple system serving two or three beds costs about 20 pounds in materials and takes an afternoon to install.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on irrigation systems (2024). Waterwise UK efficient irrigation guide. WRAP water and gardens research. Netafim drip irrigation technical documentation. Food and Agriculture Organisation drip irrigation manual for smallholders.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small plastic device attached to an irrigation pipe that releases water at a controlled rate, typically 1 to 4 litres per hour. A drip emitter delivers water to a single plant's root zone. Multiple emitters branch off a main supply line.

content.text: Gravity-fed drip irrigation works with as little as 0.3 m of head (height between the butt tap and the plant). A butt raised on a stand at 50 to 80 cm gives enough pressure for soaker hose and most drip systems up to 30 m in length. At 1 m of head, you have about 0.1 bar of pressure. This is low but sufficient for perforated soaker hose and simple
Measure the distance from the butt tap to the furthest bed. Keep this under 30 m for a gravity-fed system: beyond that, friction losses in the pipe mean inadequate flow at the far end.
Sketch the layout: one main supply line from the butt, branching into feeder lines at each bed. Keep branches as short as possible.
For a rectangular bed, run soaker hose in parallel rows 30 to 40 cm apart. Connect one end of each row to the main line and cap the other end.
15 mm or 13 mm main supply pipe (black polyethylene irrigation hose).
Soaker hose or 4 mm drip line for the bed runs.
Tee fittings, elbow fittings, and end caps (push-fit for 13 mm systems).
A simple Y-connector or timer valve at the butt tap if you want automatic or zone control.
Ground stakes to hold the pipe in place.

At 1 m of head, soaker hose delivers roughly 2 to 4 litres per metre per hour. A 3 m bed with one soaker hose run delivers 6 to 12 litres per hour. Most vegetable beds need 3 to 5 litres per square metre per watering. For a 3 m × 1.2 m bed, that is about 18 litres per watering. Run the system for 2 to 3 hours in the evening. Adjust based on how the soil feels to a finger inserted 10 cm deep: moist but not soggy.
Flush the main supply line at the start of each season to remove sediment.
Check for blocked emitters or soaker sections that are not weeping water. A section that runs dry while others run is usually blocked with silt or algae. Soak in diluted vinegar (1:10) to clear.
Drain and store the system indoors or coiled loosely in winter. Soaker hose left in the ground over a UK winter tends to split.
